did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an ensemble deep learning framework combining ConvNeXt-Tiny, EfficientNetV2-S, and MobileNetV3-Large for automated multi-class skin lesion classification on the HAM10000 dataset. It employs techniques such as balanced stratified sampling, adversarial Albumentations-based data augmentation, label smoothing, dropout regularization, and SmoothGrad for explainable AI (XAI) to identify discriminatory lesion areas.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original experimental study proposing a three-stream ensemble deep learning framework (combining ConvNeXt-Tiny, EfficientNetV2-S, and MobileNetV3-Large) for automated multi-class skin lesion classification. It details specific methodologies including balanced stratified sampling, adversarial data augmentation, and SmoothGrad for explainability, and reports original experimental results with a test accuracy of 89.72% on the HAM10000 dataset, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: An Ensemble Deep Learning Framework for Automated Multi Class Skin Lesion Classification Using ConvNeXt-Tiny, EfficientNetV2-S, and MobileNetV3
brief_summary: This study proposes a three-stream ensemble deep learning framework combining ConvNeXt-Tiny, EfficientNetV2-S, and MobileNetV3-Large for automated multi-class skin lesion classification on the HAM10000 dataset. To address class imbalance without synthetic oversampling, the authors employed a balanced stratified sampling strategy and adversarial Albumentations-based data augmentation. Each backbone model was fine-tuned using label smoothing, dropout regularization, early stopping, and adaptive learning rate scheduling, with final predictions generated via probability-level ensemble averaging. The framework also incorporated SmoothGrad for post-hoc explainability to identify diagnostically relevant lesion areas. Experimental results demonstrated that the proposed ensemble achieved a test accuracy of 89.72%, outperforming individual backbone models in both overall accuracy and macro-averaged F1-score. The authors conclude that the framework offers a robust, interpretable, and computationally efficient solution suitable for low-resource clinical settings.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven diagnostic classes in the HAM10000 dataset used for multi-class classification. The paper notes it is visually similar to benign keratosis-like lesions and often confused with Nevus.
One of the seven diagnostic classes in the HAM10000 dataset. It is the predominant class in the dataset distribution and is recognized with high accuracy across all architectures.
One of the seven diagnostic classes in the HAM10000 dataset. It is a non-melanoma skin cancer type and is visually similar to Actinic Keratoses.
One of the seven diagnostic classes in the HAM10000 dataset. It is visually similar to Basal Cell Carcinoma and Benign Keratosis-like lesions.
One of the seven diagnostic classes in the HAM10000 dataset. It is visually similar to Melanoma and Actinic Keratoses.
One of the seven diagnostic classes in the HAM10000 dataset. It is significantly underrepresented in the dataset.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class skin lesion diagnostic categories (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratoses and Intraepithelial Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis-like Lesions, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs automated multi-class skin lesion classification on the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels for seven lesion classes using probability-level ensemble averaging. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available benchmark dataset consisting of 10,015 high-resolution dermoscopic images collected from various sources and clinical settings, hosted on Harvard Dataverse. It is widely used for the detection of dermatological images.

use.training: Used for training the ensemble deep learning framework (ConvNeXt-Tiny, EfficientNetV2-S, MobileNetV3-Large) after applying balanced stratified sampling and adversarial Albumentations-based data augmentation.
use.validation: Used for validation (10% of the dataset) to monitor convergence and prevent overfitting during fine-tuning.
use.testing: Used for testing (20% of the dataset) to evaluate the final performance of the ensemble model, achieving a test accuracy of 89.72%.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To address class imbalance by ensuring equal contribution of classes in the training set without synthetic oversampling.
To improve feature diversity and generalization by simulating variations in brightness, contrast, hue, and saturation.
Used during fine-tuning to curb overfitting and improve generalization.
Used during fine-tuning to curb overfitting.
To minimize prediction variance and autocratic intentions in individual models, enhancing robustness and classification consistency across all lesion types.
To generate ensemble saliency maps for post-hoc explainability by averaging gradients over noise-perturbed inputs, identifying diagnostically relevant lesion areas.
To provide in-depth understanding of class prediction behavior, misclassification patterns (e.g., MEL vs BKL, BCC vs AKIEC), and diagonal dominance for the ensemble versus individual models.
To evaluate overall generalization and performance on underrepresented classes, addressing the limitations of individual models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the proposed ensemble and individual backbone models were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and confusion matrix analysis without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: The ensemble model achieved the highest test accuracy (89.72%) and significant improvements in macro-averaged precision and F1-score compared to individual backbone models. Individual models showed lower macro-level scores, implying insensitivity to underrepresented classes. The ensemble demonstrated better separation of classes and less misclassification in clinically critical pairs (e.g., MEL vs BKL, BCC vs AKIEC). Previous studies cited for comparison include Mahbod et al. (2023) with 86.2% accuracy, Thwin S.M. et al. (2024) with 87.9% accuracy, and Halder A. et al. (2025) with 89.3% accuracy.

architecture_used.main_model: Three-stream ensemble deep learning framework combining ConvNeXt-Tiny, EfficientNetV2-S, and MobileNetV3-Large
architecture_used.pretraining: All three backbone models were pretrained on the ImageNet database

backbones.name: ConvNeXt-Tiny
backbones.details: Inspired by transformer design principles; uses conv kernels, depth-wise separable layers, and simplified normalization schemes.

backbones.name: EfficientNetV2-S
backbones.details: Designed for training efficiency and parameter scaling; implements fused MBConv blocks, scaling of depth and width, optimization, and better regularization.

backbones.name: MobileNetV3-Large
backbones.details: Lightweight network for resource-constrained devices; uses inverted residual blocks, squeeze-and-excitation modules, and efficient nonlinearities.

architecture_details.ensemble_method: Probability-level ensemble averaging of softmax probability outputs from all three models; final prediction is the mean probability of the highest-ranked class.

training_details.stage_1: Pretrained backbone layers frozen; classification head trained to fit high-level representations.
training_details.stage_2: All layers unfrozen for end-to-end fine-tuning.
training_details.data_augmentation: Balanced stratified sampling (BalancedDataGen) and adversarial Albumentations-based data augmentation (including contrast limited adaptive histogram equalization, brightness/contrast/hue/saturation variations).

classification_or_output_head.fusion: Averaged across the three models to generate final predictions.

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing: All dermoscopic images were downsampled to a spatial resolution of 224 x 224 pixels to match the default input size of the backbone architectures (ConvNeXt-Tiny, EfficientNetV2-S, and MobileNetV3-Large).

Normalization: Pixel values were normalized based on ImageNet statistics to align the input distribution with pretrained weights, reducing distributional shift between the source domain (ImageNet) and target domain (dermoscopic images).

On-the-fly Processing: Preprocessing was implemented as part of the data creation pipeline and run on-the-fly during training and testing to avoid memory overhead and ensure efficient batch loading, transformation, and normalization.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: A custom class-balanced sampling scheme applied during training to address class imbalance without synthetic oversampling. It ensures equal contribution of classes in the training set by randomly choosing a diagnostic class and then selecting an image at random from that class for each mini-batch, maintaining the natural distribution of dermoscopic features.

Applied using the Albumentations library to increase generalization and reduce overfitting. Includes controlled geometric and photometric corrections that do not alter lesion morphology. Specific techniques include horizontal flipping, shift-scale-rotate transformations, brightness and contrast variations, hue and saturation perturbations, and contrast limited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE). Applied stochastically during training only.

conclusions: The proposed three-stream ensemble deep learning framework combining ConvNeXt-Tiny, EfficientNetV2-S, and MobileNetV3-Large achieved a test accuracy of 89.72% on the HAM10000 dataset, outperforming individual backbone models.

The ensemble model demonstrated significant improvements in macro-averaged precision (0.8756) and F1-score (0.8551) compared to individual models, indicating better generalization and sensitivity to underrepresented minority classes.

Probability-level ensemble averaging effectively minimized prediction variance and reduced misclassification in clinically critical pairs, such as Melanoma vs. Benign Keratosis-like lesions and Basal Cell Carcinoma vs. Actinic Keratoses.

The framework addresses class imbalance without synthetic oversampling by using balanced stratified sampling and adversarial Albumentations-based data augmentation, which improved feature diversity and generalization.

The integration of SmoothGrad for post-hoc explainability generated ensemble saliency maps that consistently identified lesion-specific discriminatory areas, enhancing clinical interpretability and confidence in model predictions.
The proposed solution is computationally efficient and robust, making it suitable for deployment in low-resource clinical settings and potential mobile or edge-AI screening services.
While the ensemble reduced confusion between visually similar classes, it did not completely eliminate familiarity with rare classes such as Dermatofibroma (DF) and Vascular Lesions (VASC).

Future work should focus on incorporating explicit lesion segmentation or attention mechanisms, integrating clinical metadata, and validating the framework on heterogeneous multi-center datasets and in prospective clinical studies.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which exhibits severe class imbalance (NV predominates, DF and VASC underrepresented), limiting generalizability to heterogeneous multi-center datasets.

No external validation was performed; the model was only tested on the HAM10000 test set (20% split), raising concerns about deployment performance on images from different acquisition systems or patient demographics.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (p-values) for performance comparisons between the ensemble and individual models.

While the ensemble reduced confusion between visually similar classes (e.g., MEL vs BKL), it did not fully overcome difficulties with rare classes like Dermatofibroma (DF) and Vascular Lesions (VASC).

Explainability was provided via post-hoc SmoothGrad saliency maps, but the paper acknowledges that saliency maps can be inaccurate and notes that clinical usefulness requires verification with expert annotations.

The framework addresses class imbalance using balanced stratified sampling and data augmentation rather than synthetic oversampling, which avoids synthetic artifacts but may not fully replicate natural data distribution.

The study compares results against previous literature (e.g., Mahbod et al., Thwin et al., Halder et al.) rather than against state-of-the-art baselines trained on the same split, making direct performance benchmarking difficult.
